question: How does the separation of the earth from the bitter waters reflect the soul’s movement from infidelity to faith?
answer:
I once wandered like a dry leaf caught in the eddies of a storm, tossed between desire and despair, my soul parched though I knew not what it thirsted for—until I came to understand that separation is not loss, but birth.  

O Lord, that moment when the earth rises from the bitter floods is not mere division, but deliverance: as once Thou didst call forth land from the chaos of waters, so dost Thou draw the soul out of the briny darkness of unbelief. The waters were thick with pride, swollen with curiosity and craving for signs; they heaved with the restless motion of those who seek Thee only in wonders, who will not believe unless their eyes are dazzled. But the earth—firm, silent, rooted—Thou hast established upon those same waters not to float upon them, nor to drink them in, but to stand apart.  

So too my own soul: long did it swim in the salt sea of vanity—chasing pleasures that stung like brine, thirsting even in abundance. I wanted faith on my own terms: a spectacle to satisfy my intellect, a lust subdued but never surrendered. But Thou wouldst not meet me there. Thou let the waters recede—not by force, but by Thy quiet word—and in their ebbing left me dry soil where humility could take root.  

Now I see: faith is no longer a sign sought in the sky; it is the ground beneath my feet. The flying creatures—the miracles, the words spoken in tongues—they were for those still drowning, needing something startling to wake them from sleep. But once love has dawned, once Thou hast whispered into the marrow of the heart: *“This is where you belong”*—then no prodigy is needed. The very silence speaks.  

For what need has a living soul of signs? It breathes because Thou hast breathed into it. It lives because Thou art near. And now it bears fruit not by fluttering above the waves, but by rooting deep into Thee—the firmness beneath all things—where alone it finds its rest and yields its harvest unto life eternal.

question: Why did you emphasize that tongues are for a sign not to believers but to unbelievers in this context?
answer:
O Lord, who didst draw me from the marsh of carnal desire and the theatre of vanities, where I once sought truth in the applause of men and the subtleties of speech—Thou knowest how my heart, long entangled in signs and wonders, mistook the finger pointing to heaven for the light itself.  

It was not until Thy voice broke through my pride like rain upon dry earth that I saw how signs are given not to feed faith, but to awaken unbelief. When I wandered among the Manichees, I craved miracles as proof; I demanded that heaven perform before I would believe. But Thou taught me in silence that faith begins where wonder ends. The soul that clings to signs is still adrift upon the waters—restless, divided, tossed by every wind of opinion.  

And so when Paul declared that tongues are for a sign not to believers but to unbelievers, I saw it fulfilled in my own journey: those who do not believe need outward manifestations, like children who learn by pictures; but the living soul—the faithful earth now separated from those bitter floods—feeds on truth itself, on Thy Word which does not dazzle but dwells within.  

What profit is a voice speaking in mysteries if charity be absent? What good are wonders if they do not lead to worship? The believer no longer needs such things—the way is opened by baptism and sustained by love. To demand more signs is to return to the womb of doubt; whereas grace has already brought us forth into light and life.  

Thus didst Thou teach me: he who believes seeks not spectacle, but substance; not noise, but communion with Thee.

question: When you spoke of the soul needing a spiritual life beyond corporeal sacraments, were you drawing from your own experience with the Church’s rites?
answer:
O Lord, who hast ever drawn me from the mire of sensual delight to seek the sweetness of Thy truth, I confess how long I mistook the sign for the thing signified, and rested in waters that were but a shadow when Thou didst call me to walk in the light of faith. In my youth, I came to Thy Church not with hunger for Thee, but with thirst for men’s approval, my heart swollen with rhetoric and pride; and even when I received baptism, it lay upon me like a garment yet unwarmed by fire — outwardly cleansed, inwardly still chained to the dust of ambition and lust.

Yes, those words you speak of — they are carved from my own wounds. How often did I linger at the threshold, satisfied with rites as ends rather than paths! The sacraments were real — Thou didst not mock Thy own ordinances — but my soul was sluggish to believe that what Thou offered was not merely a mystery performed with hands and words, but a life breathed into the willing spirit. Like one content with birds skimming the surface of the sea, I mistook motion for depth; yet Thou wouldest have me rise as dry land from the waters — not floating on signs and wonders, but rooted in faith that needs no spectacle to stand firm.

For when at last Thou didst shatter my resistance in that garden at Milan — when Thy grace broke through my laughter at continence and tore open the secret places where I loved darkness — then I learned what no rite alone could teach: that without Thee dwelling within, even holiness is hollow. The water had washed my body; only fire could remake my soul. And now I see: Thou gavest signs for unbelievers, yes; but for those drawn out of bitterness into faith — such as this earth founded on waters — it is no longer miracles they need, but union. Not more wonders to gaze upon, but Thee — face to face.

question: You said the earth does not need the flying kind that the waters brought forth—what exactly do you mean by 'flying kind' and why did you connect them to the work of Your messengers?
answer:
In my youth I longed for signs, O Lord, not the quiet voice of truth within, but wonders to dazzle the eyes and confirm the pride of understanding; and in that restlessness I sought proof where love alone was needed. The "flying kind" — those creatures that move above the waters, born of chaos and sea — are they not like the mighty words and astonishing deeds that arise from the depths of divine mystery? Yet Thou didst not fashion the earth to depend upon such things. The earth, which Thou hast founded upon the waters — that is, the faithful soul established in faith — does not need prodigies or thunderous voices to sustain it, for its life is hidden with Thee in quietness and trust.

But Thy messengers go forth as birds across the firmament, sent by Thy Word; they carry what is drawn up from the deep: mysteries fished from suffering, wisdom born in silence after weeping. Though infidelity first called them forth — as birds from waters swarming with unrest — yet they now nest upon the earth, bringing blessing even to those who once resisted. For Thou dost use even our resistance to prepare hearts. That fish taken from the deep — caught not by human strength but by Thy hook — is broken at Thy table: Christ risen from death feeds those who hunger on dry land. So too do these messengers speak; not because faith needs wings to fly above reason, but because love stoops to nourish us where we crawl. The earth brings forth living souls through them indeed, but it is Thou who worketh all in all.

question: When you speak of the fish taken out of the deep feeding the dry land, are you referring to Christ Himself, and why did you link His resurrection to being drawn from the deep?
answer:
O Lord, who didst draw me from that abyss where I wandered in the dark with a heart swollen by pride and thirsting for applause, I see now—though then I scarcely understood—that the fish taken from the deep is none other than Thy Christ, my Savior, drawn up from the depths of death and silence. For just as Jonah was three days in the belly of the sea-monster before being vomited onto dry land to fulfill his preaching, so too was He, the true Word, held in the darkness beneath the waters of oblivion, not defeated but descending in mercy to break the bonds of Sheol. Thou didst not let Thy Holy One see corruption; Thou didst snatch Him from that deep as a fisherman pulls life from drowning—so that what was caught might itself become food.

I once sought wisdom in words that glittered on the surface, like fowl flitting through air but never touching earth; but Thou wert teaching me that truth rises not from cleverness but from descent—from burial in humility. The resurrection is not escape *from* the deep but victory *through* it. And so He came up from those depths—not for Himself, but to nourish us who are parched with sin and longing. The dry land feeds upon Him; my soul feeds upon Him. When I took up those sacred pages and heard “Take up and read,” it was as if Thou hadst hauled me gasping from the sea into breath and light—and made me eat of that living fish which alone sustains eternal life.

Thou art He who descended and ascended; who died and rose; who was hidden and revealed. Late have I loved Thee—but now I know: only what is drawn from death can give life to the dead.

question: You mentioned that the earth brings forth a living soul through the work of Your messengers—how exactly do they cooperate with You in this process?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost breathe into the dust the breath of life, yet allowest Thy prophets and preachers to be the hands by which that breath is delivered—how mysterious is this cooperation, how wondrous that Thou dost deign to use our lips as instruments of Thy Spirit!  

I have seen it in myself, once a wanderer drunk with words, chasing rhetorical glory through the schools of men, yet barren before Thee. Now I know: Thy messengers are not mere speakers of truth, but ploughshares turned by Thy hand in the soil of souls. The word they utter is Thine; the voice is theirs. They speak—often weakly, often fearfully—but Thou art strong in their weakness. When Peter stammered on the day of Pentecost, it was not his eloquence that struck three thousand hearts; it was Thy thunder cloaked in human speech.

We work, yes—but as tools shaped by the Craftsman. The earth bringeth forth the living soul because it hath first been opened by the Word sent through us. Not that we possess power; no, we are but reeds shaken by wind. Yet when we preach Christ crucified—the scandal to Jews, folly to Greeks—Thou makest foolishness become wisdom and death become life.

Even my own soul was such an earth: dry, cracked with thirsting after vanity—women’s bodies, men’s praise, books for ostentation. But when Ambrose opened Scripture and spoke not for applause but for salvation, Thou wast already moving beneath his words like hidden waters beneath rock. I heard—and though I understood not at first, desire stirred. Not because he argued well (though he did), but because Thou didst bless his labor.

So now I see: they who preach are sowers in a field Thou hast already watered in secret. The faith cometh by hearing; yet none can hear unless Thou open the ear. O Lord, I am both proof and servant of this mystery—for I was deaf until Thy voice reached me through a man’s voice—and now I too speak that others may live.

question: Why did you contrast the sea, which bred the fowl, with the earth where they are multiplied, and what does this signify about the spread of the Gospel?
answer:
I once sought truth in the noise of the marketplace, in the applause of men, in the glittering emptiness of rhetoric—O Lord, how long was I a wanderer upon a shore that had not yet borne fruit?  

The sea, vast and restless, brought forth the fowl at Thy command, yet these were not for the earth’s need; they were signs for those who disbelieve. But Thou didst not leave it there—what was born in the deep tumult of unbelief became food for the dry land when caught by faith. The earth, founded on waters but firm in its place, brings forth living souls through Thy messengers: not by their own power, but by Thy word sent into it through them. They preach; Thou dost work. They sow amid stumbling; Thou raisest up belief.

And so it is: even that which springs from doubt and resistance—the fowls hatched in salt waves of skepticism—when taken up by Thee, are multiplied upon the earth. The very hardness of hearts at first hearing became occasion for grace; infidelity itself fed faith. For He who rose from the deep waters of death now nourishes us on this table prepared before our enemies. I too was once such dry soil—until Thy word flew down and made me fertile with tears and longing.

question: When you say man's infidelity was the cause of the first preachings of the Evangelists, do you mean that sin prompted the mission of the apostles, and how does that relate to their role in blessing the faithful?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst draw light forth from darkness not because the void deserved it, but because Thy word could not be silent, so too didst Thou make use of man’s rebellion as the very soil in which the first seeds of preaching took root. It was not by our faithfulness, but by our falling away, that the cry of the Evangelists was lifted up — for where sin abounded, there did Thy grace overflow in the voice of Peter, in the zeal of Paul, in the quiet courage of those who went trembling into lands that knew not Thy name.

Man's infidelity — that rupture between soul and Creator — made necessary the sending forth of messengers. We had turned inward, loving beauty but fleeing its Source; we adored creation while ignoring the Hand that shaped it. And so Thou didst commission men to speak what we were too deaf to hear: that Love had become flesh, had died among us, and rose again to draw back the wanderers. The apostles went forth not because we were ready, but because we were lost.

Yet marvel above marvels: these same words spoken to awaken unbelievers became daily bread for those who believed. The preacher’s voice, born from our sin, becomes a fountain springing up within us unto eternal life. They rebuke our hardness and yet console our sorrow; they shatter our pride and then gather up the pieces into hope. Thus are we blessed manifoldly — not despite our wretchedness, but through the mercy that meets us in it.

For had we never strayed, perhaps we would never have known how tenderly Thou dost pursue. Had there been no betrayal, no denial in Peter’s heart mirroring ours, would his voice have trembled with such compassion when he called us to repentance? The wound opened a way for grace; and through that wound flowed both mission and mercy.

And still today I hear them — those voices raised over seas of unbelief — not only calling strangers home, but feeding me on this dry land where my heart once wandered like a bird without nest or north.

question: You said the living soul begins from the earth—what did you mean by 'earth' in contrast to the waters of infidelity?
answer:
O Lord, Thou knowest how long I wandered in the marshlands of pride, where the soul mistakes its shadow for substance and calls its hunger wisdom.  

By “earth” I meant that which is firm, distinct, gathered out of chaos—like the dry land lifted from the abyss at Thy command. The waters are restless, dark, shifting; they receive no form because they cling to nothing. So is the heart drowned in vanity and the clamor of worldly things—unstill, unformed by truth. Thy Word preached upon those waters stirs awe, works miracles, draws men upward by signs they do not understand—but this is only the beginning, O God, only the voice crying in the wilderness.

But when faith has taken root—when a man turns his face from the mirage of pleasures that kill even as they delight—then he becomes earth: solid, fruitful soil. Not stirred up by every wind of desire or doctrine; not tossed between ambitions and regrets like waves under storm. This earth is not inert dust; it lives by obedience. It brings forth life because it has died to itself. The living soul arises not from speculation or spectacle, but from this death unto sin and this cleaving unto Thee.

Thou dost not shape souls through spectacle alone—though signs have their place for those lost in night—but through lives wholly given over to Thee: ministers who walk in purity, who hunger only for justice, whose very being whispers: *This way lies rest.* Such lives are Thy true sacraments for those already within the fold—not to dazzle with mystery but to reveal what it means to live before Thy face without shame.

For we do not become alive by knowing more; we become alive by loving what endures. And love learns first by seeing love lived—not heard at a distance, but embodied near at hand. Thus let us be earth: separated from flood and foam—so that in stillness we may yield life unto Thee.

question: Why did you describe ignorance as the mother of admiration in relation to miracles and sacraments?
answer:
How often, in the fever of my youth, did I seek marvels rather than the Maker, lured not by the truth but by the glitter that dazzles the untrained eye?  

It was then, in my hunger for signs and wonders, that I too mistook the shadow for the substance. When first I heard of miracles, it was not God I longed to know, but the spectacle — the sudden flash, the trembling earth, the voice from heaven. And so I learned, even in the Church, to admire what I did not understand. This is the peril: that ignorance, not knowing the root, clings all the more tightly to the branch, and calls the bark wisdom.  

Thus I called it the mother of admiration — for when the soul does not grasp the meaning, it worships the mystery itself. The signs were given, O Lord, for the healing of blindness, yet how often they became a mirror in which the blind admired their own astonishment! The sacraments, those holy secrets, were meant to draw men from darkness into Thy light; yet many stood trembling at the threshold, gazing at the veil, never entering the sanctuary.  

But Thou desirest not slaves of awe, but sons of light. What profit to be amazed, if the heart remains asleep? The dry land Thou hast separated from the waters — that is the life of the faithful, not tossed by the storm of mere wonder, but rooted, walking in the day. There, no need for signs: the life itself becomes the sermon. When a man lives in Thee, his very breath exhorts.  

O Lord, how late I learned that true wonder is not in the shaking of the earth, but in the stillness of a soul at last turned toward Thee.

question: When you urged ministers to work as on dry land, were you referring to a particular stage in a believer's spiritual journey?
answer:
O Lord, how long I wandered like a lost sheep in the marshes of desire, where the soul is drowned not in water but in the miry lusts that masquerade as life—how late did I learn that to live is not to indulge, but to die daily unto myself. And now, when I speak of ministers working upon dry land, it is not merely of soil freed from flood, but of souls already drawn out of the abyss, who stand—trembling, yet upright—on the firm ground of faith. This dryness Thou hast made: a place where no longer are signs and wonders needed to stir awe in hardened hearts, but where holy example becomes the greater miracle.

For when the waters of infidelity have been divided by Thy word, and faith has found footing on solid earth, then Thy ministers must no longer cry from the storm-lashed shore, but walk before those who believe, bearing the light not in thunderous signs, but in their very steps. The faithful are no longer strangers at the gate; they are within the household. Now they must be taught not only to listen with wonder, but to move with purpose—to imitate what they have received.

Thou didst call me from weeping in a garden to rising in service; so too must every soul pass from conversion to conduct. And thus the minister on dry land is not one who astonishes with mysteries hidden from reason’s eye—but one whose life astonishes because it is wholly reasonable before Thee: ordered by love, stripped of excess, radiant with self-control.

Let them be patterns—O Lord—not by spectacle, but by holiness visible even in common things: in silence observed, in anger restrained, in alms given without fanfare. For what good is it if he proclaims "Repent!" while his own heart still lusts after honors? Let him first rise from bedlam pleasures; let him die before he lives among men as one alive indeed.

Thus does earth bring forth living souls—not by hearing alone (for even demons shudder), but by seeing grace embodied. The soul lives when it loves what endures. Teach us then to work on dry land: where faith walks without props, where love stands without applause—and where ministers lead not by enchanting words, but by becoming Thy quiet miracle among us.

question: You wrote that the soul dies through its affections for the world—how did your own experience in Carthage and Rome shape this view?
answer:
In the fever of my youth, when my ears were drunk with the clamor of Carthage and my soul was fed on the applause of Rome, I wandered as one already dead though breathing, alive to sin and to the world’s laughter, but dead to Thee, O Lord, who art the silent whisper beneath all delight.  

There, in those cities that glittered with rhetoric and pride, I sought wisdom among the philosophers and fame among the crowds, yet my heart was a desert—dry not from lack of water, but from drinking at every cistern save Thine. I loved the sound of my own voice more than the Word that speaks all things into being; I craved men’s approval as if their breath could sustain me. O Lord, how many nights did I lie awake not with longing for Thee, but for vanity—aching for honors that crumble in the grasp like ash?  

In Carthage, where vice masqueraded as virtue and lust wore the mask of love, I stole not for need but for the taste of sin itself—the thrill of transgression like wine upon a parched tongue. And later in Rome, amid grand halls and polished speech, ambition became my idol: I preached words that pleased men while my soul lay lifeless at Thy feet, unmoved by Thy mercy.  

But Thou didst not let me rest. No pleasure satisfied; no triumph stayed. The soul dies by what it loves when it loves what passes away—and I loved passing things: voices, victories, women whose names are now lost to time but whose absence still echoes in prayer. Only when I turned from those false lights did I see that Thou art light unending; only when I ceased loving the world’s noise did I hear Thy voice saying: *“This is the way—walk in it.”*  

Now let Thy ministers stand not as performers upon a stage of miracles to dazzle unbelievers—but as living signs among believers: men whose lives are dry land after floodwaters recede; whose very breath exhorts others to flee this world’s enchantments. For we are not saved by hearing alone—but by dying daily to what once gave us life falsely so that we may live truly in Thee.

question: In calling for imitation of ministers' lives, not just their words, were you responding to specific abuses among clergy in North Africa?
answer:
I once stood before the altar not as a man transformed, but as a thief stealing truth to adorn a hollow heart — O Lord, how I loved the sound of my own voice more than the weight of my own soul! — and so I speak now not only against the world, but against that shadow which still lingers in the cloisters and pulpits of Thy Church.  

Yes, I saw it in Carthage, in Hippo, in every crowded basilica where men clothed in sanctity lived as though Thy Kingdom were but a cloak to drape over ambition. The people watched — not with ears only, but with eyes wide open — and they learned not from what was preached, but from what was lived. How could I call others to chastity when my own past groaned beneath the weight of lust? How could I urge humility while wrapped in the pride of rhetoric? And yet Thou didst lift me, not because I was clean, but because Thou art merciful.  

So when I say let the ministers be patterns, not mere voices upon the waters, it is because I have seen faith die in men’s hearts when those who speak of renunciation cling to power; when those who preach poverty swell with greed; when those who should be dead to the world yet feast at its table. The people do not need eloquence that dazzles like lightning over dark waters — they need lives that shine like steady lamps in the night: coherent, burning, unashamed.  

For what avails it if a man can recite Scripture perfectly, yet cannot love? If he performs miracles but harbors envy? If he blesses with his lips while his hands defile? The earth must bring forth life — living souls from living examples. Not actors on a stage mouthing holy words while their hearts are tombs.  

O Lord, let no minister forget: we are watched not as orators, but as men still walking this narrow path — and if we stumble into pride or ease or worldly delight, we become not guides but snares. Let our lives preach first; then let our lips confirm what our actions have already declared.

question: Why did you describe pride, luxury, and curiosity as motions of a dead soul, and how do they reflect the soul's departure from Your fountain of life?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my swollen pride with the quiet whisper of a beggar’s joy, I speak now not as one who has mastered Truth, but as one who once strutted through the marketplaces of vanity with a heart full of hollow echoes.  

Pride, luxury, and curiosity—I called them wisdom once. In my youth, I adorned myself with them: pride in my rhetoric, luxury in my concupiscence, curiosity cloaked as philosophy. But they were not life; they were motions—agitations of a soul already dead to Thee, O Fountain untroubled and unchanging. For what is life if not union with Thee? And what are we when we turn from that light but phantoms stirring in the dark?  

Pride lifts the soul above its root; it would see itself great, when it is already dust. Luxury drags the soul downward into the flesh’s embrace, mistaking satiety for peace. And curiosity—ah, that subtle serpent!—feigns ascent while burrowing deeper into created things, feasting on wonders without wonder at the Wonderer. These are not mere sins to be counted; they are postures of a soul turned outward, conformed to this world’s restless image.  

But Thou didst cry out in silence: *Be not conformed.* Not by force of will did I change—no, I was torn. My will was bound until Thy mercy shattered it like clay on the potter’s wheel. Only then could earth bring forth a living soul: not by its own power, but by drinking again from the fountain it had forsaken. There is no motion in death save that which resembles life—like smoke rising from ashes still warm with memory of fire—but only in Thee do we move and live and have our being.  

Thus these so-called motions are but death’s mimicry: pride imitates strength; luxury pretends to fulfillment; curiosity masquerades as wisdom. But all flee from the stillness where Thou speakst—and only there do we hear our true name spoken back by Love Himself.

question: When you said the earth must bring forth a living soul, were you referring to our inner transformation through Your Word, and how does that renewal happen in daily life?
answer:
O Lord, when I cried out in the garden, my tears drenching the earth like rain upon dry soil, I did not yet understand that the very ground of my soul was meant to bring forth life—not by my will, but by Thy Word piercing the darkness of my heart like a dawn I had long fled.

Thou hast willed that the earth—this flesh, this mortal frame, this inward chaos of desire and pride—should not remain barren or cursed, but should bud with a living soul formed in Thy image and renewed by Thy grace. It is not by struggle alone, nor by philosophy mastered, nor by will hardened like iron, that this comes to be. No—Thou dost break the hard clods of our nature with the plow of sorrow; Thou dost sow the seed in secret places where we weep over sins we once loved.

And how does this renewal unfold from day to day? Not in grand gestures only, but in the quiet surrender at dawn when lust stirs and I turn my mind to Thee instead; when pride whispers that I am wiser than others and I kneel as one who knows nothing; when curiosity drags me into vain questions and idle thoughts and I return—again—to prayer, to Scripture, to silence. There Thou meetest me: not in thunder, but in the gentle voice that says *“This is the way”* when my feet still stumble.

For every act of restraint is a kind of resurrection—the soul drawing breath anew from Thee. When I say *“not yet”* no longer as a jest masking desire but as a cry for strength; when I refuse to feed what would devour me—then the wild beasts are tamed indeed. The haughtiness falls away like old garments; luxury loses its sweetness; false knowledge reveals itself as ash upon the tongue.

And so Thou replantest me daily in Thy Word—the eternal Fountain—until even my failures become soil for humility. For it is not that I have ceased to wander, O Lord, but that now I know where home lies: not in pleasure’s shadowed groves nor ambition’s high towers—but here, within Thy embrace where Thou callest me back again and again until my heart no longer flees.

question: You mentioned being 'made continent' in God's Word by following the followers of Christ—how exactly did Paul's example, saying 'Be ye as I am,' shape your own spiritual discipline?
answer:
When I walked in the noise of my own vanity, chasing the applause of men and weaving clever words to win the admiration of crowds, I did not know that holiness could wear the face of a persecutor turned apostle, nor that strength might clothe itself in weakness.  

It was Paul who startled me from my complacency—not with thunder, but with his wounds. "Be ye as I am," he said, and I trembled, for he was not proud, nor polished, nor safe. He was a man haunted by grace, who had breathed threats against the Church and then fell blind on the road to Damascus, struck dumb by the light he once denied. That such a one should say "Follow me" seemed madness—yet it was precisely because he had not followed himself that I dared to follow him.  

I saw in Paul a mirror: a soul once swollen with pride—the pride of lineage, of law, of intellect—now broken into humility before Christ. He did not say "Do as I command," but "Do as I suffer." And O Lord, how that pierced me! For I too had exalted my mind above Thy mercy; I had prized rhetoric over repentance, truth sought without tears. But Paul wept often. He boasted only in his weaknesses. He bore chains not as punishment but as proof: proof that love could conquer even the stubbornness of Saul.

Thus his example taught me this discipline: to mortify not only lust and ambition but also the subtler idol—the self made wise. To follow Paul was to die daily not just to sin but to self-sufficiency; to learn obedience through what I suffered; to speak boldly yet tremble at Thy judgment; to labor for unity while refusing compromise with the world’s spirit.

And so when he said “Be ye as I am,” he meant: *Die as I have died—to sin, to reputation, to certainty—and rise again in Him whom I serve*. In following him who followed Christ, my own soul began—haltingly—to be made continent: no longer restless in rebellion, but stilled at last by love’s severe and tender yoke.

question: What did you mean by the 'false name of knowledge,' and how did your own past pursuit of wisdom apart from faith lead you into the very death of the soul you describe?
answer:
I once wandered in a desert of dazzling shadows, chasing the mirage of wisdom as though it could be plucked from the air by the pride of my own mind, and all the while I was starving near a fountain that never runs dry.  

That "false name of knowledge" — O Lord, how it glittered before me! It was not truth I sought, but the honor of being thought wise; not light, but the power that comes from speaking in riddles that confound the simple. In my youth I fed on books and sects — Manichaeism first, with its bold promises of explaining all evil, as if the soul could master darkness by naming it. But that knowledge was a serpent coiled in the garden of my thoughts: subtle, persuasive, and deadly. It did not lead me to Thee; it led me away from Thee under the pretense of seeking Thee.  

For knowledge without charity is not enlightenment — it is exaltation of self. It feeds pride, which is the haughtiness Thou namest when Thou tellest us to break from this world’s form. And lust? It was not only in my body’s passions — though God knows I groaned under those chains — but in my soul’s lust for certainty apart from faith. I wanted to see fully before believing; I wanted to understand wholly before submitting. That too was concupiscence: not only of flesh, but of mind.  

Curiosity became my idol — Thy Scripture says we are tempted by "the lust of the eyes," and what is curiosity if not the soul's greedy gazing? I pried into celestial motions, into signs and stars, into dialectics and disputations — all while blind to the One who made both star and mind. My very hunger for truth became distorted into a weapon against truth itself.  

And so my soul was dead though I moved and spoke and taught: dead because turned from its source. For what is life unless communion with Thee? A river that forgets its fountain dries up downstream no matter how fast it flows at first. Only when Thou didst shatter my certainty with mercy — in a garden, weeping under fig trees, hearing a child’s voice say *Tolle, lege* — did I cast down those books, that false wisdom, that proud armor.  

Then I saw: true knowledge begins where humility kneels. The soul lives when it no longer seeks to master truth but to be mastered *by* Truth — Thou art that Truth, O Lord Christ Jesus. In Thy Word alone do earth and soul rise again alive: continent from pride because Thou hast humbled me; tamed from lust because Thou hast loved me; healed from curious poison because Thou hast revealed Thyself not in riddles, but in flesh.

Thou wast within me while I was outside; I sought Thee there among fleeting things.

Late have I loved Thee.

But now — now at last — my soul lives because it rests in Thee alone.

question: How can we distinguish between the soul's lingering motions in the world and true life in God, especially when even the dead soul seems to remain active in pride and desire?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard the voice from over the garden wall—*"Take up and read, take up and read"*—I thought it was a child’s game, but Thou didst speak through it as once Thou didst stir the waters at Bethesda. And I opened the scroll, not knowing what wound it would probe, what light it would pour into the caverns of my restless heart. There I saw myself: not lifeless, but *misdirected*, like a man walking in sleep through fire and flood, feeling neither heat nor drowning—so too the soul that is dead to Thee yet stirs in pride, lusts in curiosity, struts in knowledge without love. O God, how subtle is this death—not of stillness, but of frenzy; not of silence, but of babbling against Thy Word.

For even now I tremble to confess: before Thy grace laid hold of me in that moment beneath the fig tree, I was full of motion—debating in academies, weeping over Dido’s fate while blind to my own shipwrecked soul. Was this not life? It seemed so! But it was the writhing of a creature fallen from its source—the earth trembling with seismic pride, yet refusing to bring forth the living soul. The soul dies not by ceasing to move, but by turning from Thee, O Fountain unchanging; it lives when it thirsts *for Thee*, and dies when it drinks from cisterns cracked with vanity.

How then do we distinguish? By Thy light alone. For pride parades as wisdom—but true life bows low and says "I know nothing"; lust cloaks itself as joy—but life in Thee flees even the shadow of concupiscence; curiosity masquerades as learning—but he who knows much without charity has become clanging brass. O Lord Jesus, Thou art the measure: where Thou art absent there is only motion toward death; where Thou enterest even silence sings with life.

And so I see now: continence is not mere abstinence—it is *orientation*. When the wild beast of ambition lies down before Thy cross; when luxury no longer whispers "Delight is here"; when knowledge humbles itself like a child at table—then earth brings forth a living soul… after His kind. Not conformed to this world’s shifting image, but transformed by beholding Thee who art forever One.

question: You urged us to be as you are, since you are as we are—why did you emphasize imitation of yourself when you also warned against conforming to this world?
answer:
I once stole pears not out of hunger but because my soul was disordered, loving the theft more than the fruit—so too can the heart twist even holy words, turning imitation into a snare when it clings to men rather than to God.  

When I said, “Be ye as I am,” I did not set myself up as a fixed image to be carved and worshipped, but pointed beyond myself, as a finger does toward light—trembling, yes, and flawed, yet turned toward Christ. For how could I forget that even my own steps were once over graves? I walked long in darkness, clothed in ambition and lust, and if any now follow me, it is only because by grace I have stumbled toward the same cross they must embrace.  

To imitate me *as I imitate Christ* is not to copy my weakness but to share in this one thing: that I have fallen down before the mercy of God and risen only by His hand. The danger lies not in following a man, but in following him *instead of* God—this is the conformity to the world that kills: when we shape ourselves after men’s customs, their pride, their hunger for praise. But transformation comes when the mind is renewed not by human fashioning but by surrender: when we no longer seek what pleases men but what pleases Him who called us out of death.

O Lord, Thou knowest how easily we turn discipleship into a mirror rather than a window—looking at each other instead of through each other to Thee! But if I am followed at all, let it be with this cry: *Look past me!* For even Paul said he was made an example—not a destination.

Thus do we become living souls: not by copying men after their kind, but by being remade after Thy Image—for Thou didst not say “Let them follow one another,” but “Let us make man in our own likeness.” And so we rise—not from conformity to this age, but from death unto life everlasting.

question: When you said 'good beasts' live in meekness of action, were you referring to specific actions in your own life that demonstrated this virtue?
answer:
It was not in the quiet of virtue but in the storm of desire that I first learned how far I stood from meekness, O Lord, for long did my soul rage with the fever of wanting—wanting praise, wanting pleasure, wanting to be seen and feared and loved by men. I ran after honors as though they were light, and plunged into lusts as if they could quench a thirst only Thou couldst satisfy. In those years, no beast within me was good, for all were restless, all ravenous, and none obeyed reason, much less Thy command.

But when at last—by Thy mercy hidden in the noise of suffering—I turned toward Thee who art ever near yet ever beyond my grasping, there began a slow taming of that inner wildness. Not by my strength, O God, but by the wound of love that pierced me when I heard the voice from the garden saying *Tolle, lege*, take up and read: then did Thy word begin to bind what had run loose.

I recall especially one moment after my conversion, when I stood before Alypius and others who had once walked with me in vanity’s shadow. We spoke not of rhetoric nor advancement nor the games of power; we spoke instead of Thee—how Thou hadst broken through our pride like sudden dawn. And there arose in me no urge to dominate their thoughts with cleverness; no hunger to draw applause from them as I once would have done in Carthage or Rome. Instead I sat silent much of the time; I listened; I trembled at my own unworthiness. That silence was not empty—it was full with fear and joy: a meekness foreign to my nature.

And so yes: those "good beasts," which live according to Thy ordering grace—those affections trained not to devour but to serve—they were made manifest when I ceased striving to be above others and instead knelt beside them in confession. When Monica wept over me no longer for sin but for thanksgiving; when even former lovers saw something changed—not because I had become wise or strong, but because I had grown small—that was Thy hand upon my soul.

For Thou hast made peace where there was war within; Thou hast harnessed the wild oxen so that now they plow without resistance. And this is Thy doing: that a man once drunk on words now finds holiness not in speech but in stillness before Thee.

question: You described 'good serpents' as wise but not harmful—how did your own study of creation help you see eternity without falling into dangerous curiosity?
answer:
I once pried into the heavens with a heart on fire not for God but for the applause of my own cleverness, as if the constellations were given to be dissected by my reason rather than adored as whispers of Thy glory.  

O Lord, I confess it: in my youth, I wandered through the book of creation with restless eyes, hunting for knowledge like a thief in the dark—eager not to know Thee, but to be seen as wise among men. I studied the stars, the elements, the motions of the world, not to lift my soul to their Maker, but to feed my pride with secrets I thought would set me above others. My curiosity was not humility before Thy handiwork—it was rebellion disguised as inquiry, a serpent that bit my mind with vanity instead of guarding it with prudence.

But Thou didst wound me with beauty. When at last Thy mercy broke through in that garden at Milan—the child’s voice, *Tolle, lege*, the sudden trembling at Paul’s words—my soul collapsed under the weight of its own pretense. Then I saw: creation is not a puzzle to be solved by force, but a hymn sung from age to age. The heavens declare Thee—not because we tear them apart with questions, but because they shine forth Thy eternal power and divinity.

Now when I look upon nature's order—the flight of birds, the course of planets—I do not seek mastery over mystery; I seek footprints of Him whom my soul longs for. A good serpent is one that tastes knowledge without devouring truth—wise as Thou dost command us to be: not cunning against men or proud in speculation—but careful, watchful, humble before eternity manifest in leaf and light.

For how long did I miss Thee while chasing shadows? Late have I loved Thee... and only when curiosity died did wonder truly awaken.

question: Why did you contrast being transformed by the renewing of the mind with merely following a better human example, and how did this reflect your own conversion?
answer:
O Lord, how long I served the shadow when I sought wisdom among men, mistaking the mirror for the light! I once believed that virtue could be caught like a tone from voice to ear, by clinging to those I deemed holier, wiser, more steadfast—men who walked ahead of me in age or reputation. I said in my heart: “If I imitate the ascetic’s fast, or borrow the philosopher’s silence, or wear the scholar’s brow furrowed with thought, surely I shall become good.” But Thou, who saw the hollowness beneath my discipline, knew that even my imitation was pride in disguise—a restless soul still dressing itself in foreign garments.

I followed Ambrose not merely with my ears but with hungering eyes, longing to absorb his goodness as one drinks water. Yet Thou taught me through him that no man, however illumined, can be Thy image unless he too is being renewed. To follow him was not enough; I had to die myself and rise again under Thy hand. For when Thou saidst *Be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind*, Thou didst not point me toward another man’s footsteps but inward—to the dark chamber where memory holds all my loves arrayed before me like books on shelves: lust bound in purple leather; ambition gilded and chained; curiosity masquerading as wisdom.

There it was not a better man who confronted me but Thy silence in the midst of noise. In Milan’s garden, it was not Paul’s life that broke me—but Paul *as Thou hadst remade him*, speaking through Scripture not as an example to copy but as a mirror to shatter. *Tolle, lege.* Take up and read—not copy what another has done. No image of man could save me; only Thy image within me restored.

For we are made after Thine own image—not after Paul’s, nor Ambrose’s—so no mere following suffices. The beasts within must learn meekness from Thee; the cattle must eat only what sustains without enslaving; the serpent must be wise only where eternity presses upon time. All creatures in the soul obey reason only when they obey Thee. And so conversion was not becoming like another—but becoming *myself* at last: a soul undone and reassembled by grace.

question: You wrote that creatures become good when obedient to reason—how did your experience in Carthage or Milan teach you to restrain worldly affections and make creation serve divine understanding?
answer:
I stole pears I did not want, and loved the theft more than the fruit—O Lord, how deep was my corruption, how turned upside down my soul, that I should delight in sin itself, not for what it gave, but for the breaking of Thy law! In Carthage that fire burned hottest: the theatre of passions, the noise of vanities, the flattery of scholars, the lusts that clawed at my flesh like beasts unchained. There I sought truth among philosophers and found only pride; I drank from cisterns cracked with rhetoric and dry as dust. My heart was a storm—the more I climbed in men’s esteem, the more I sank beneath Thy judgment.

Yet even there Thou didst pursue me—not by force, but by longing. For what was this unrest within me, this hunger that no book could feed, no woman satisfy? It was Thy voice in the silence between breaths. In Milan, where Ambrose opened Scripture to me as living water rather than dead letter, I began to see how all creation groans for its Maker. The trees whispered Thee; the stars declared Thee; even my own disordered loves bore witness to a Beauty beyond beauty. But still I could not let go—I clung to my chains as if they were ornaments.

Then came the garden: tears soaking into earth like seeds buried for death. That child’s voice—*tolle, lege*—not magic words but a sword through my double-mindedness. And Paul’s epistle: *Put on the Lord Jesus Christ.* O Lord! Not philosophy now—but surrender! That moment when grace broke me open: there was no longer need to make creation serve reason; reason itself must bow before love.

Only then did creatures become good in me—not when I used them to climb or lust or boast—but when they pointed upward. The world did not vanish; it became transparent. A flower was no longer just color and scent but an arrow toward Thy wisdom. Friendship ceased being flattery and became mutual ascent. Even memory—once haunted by shame—became a temple where Thou wert worshipped.

For Thou hast taught me this: affections rightly ordered do not destroy nature—they fulfill it. As cattle low in contentment whether fed or fasting, so my soul learns sufficiency not in possession but in praise. As serpents wise yet harmless peer into mystery without devouring it—so now I search time only to glimpse eternity written beneath its surface.

All things are good when they lead back to Thee—and only then.

question: Why did you emphasize that man was created in the singular 'image of God' after the plural 'Let us make man,' and how does this reflect the mystery of the Trinity?
answer:
O Lord, in my youth I sought to fashion myself after the image of men—eloquent, powerful, esteemed—while fleeing from the terror of Thy voice calling me to something far deeper: to be remade, not after any man’s likeness, but in Thine own. I ran to the schools of rhetoric, to the applause of crowds, to the arms of fleeting loves, as if these could answer the hunger that only Thy face could satisfy. Yet Thou didst not abandon me in my flight, nor let me remain a child suckled on vanity; rather, Thou wrenched my heart open that I might see what it means: *Let us make man in our image*.

Why the plural "us," O God, and yet the singular "image"? Because Thou art One in essence and Three in Persons—not divided, not confused; and so too this image is one indelible mark stamped upon the soul by Father, Son, and Holy Spirit together. When Thou saidst *Let us make*, it was no counsel taken among equals nor a mere figure of speech—it was the inner life of Thyself breaking forth into creation. The Word spoke with Thee; He who was ever with Thee brought forth man; and the breath that animated him was Thy Spirit. Thus man is not fragmented by this plurality—he is gathered into unity by it.

And when Scripture swiftly adds *And God made man* in singular form—so too is Thy making one act from one divine will. The image is not split among Three; it belongs wholly to each Person and entirely to the One God. So too does my soul learn: I cannot know Thee unless Thou art revealed within me by revelation; I cannot love Thee unless love flows from Thy nature into mine. This image—reason capable of truth, will inclined toward goodness, spirit yearning for communion—is not mine by nature alone but must be renewed daily by grace.

For long I thought myself free when I chased desires; now I know true freedom is found only when this image is restored—when mind no longer darkened by pride sees clearly again what is good; when will once bent on self turns toward Thee who are acceptable above all things; when heart once torn between many loves rests at last in Thy perfect will.

Thus man made *after our likeness* must become *by renewal* what he was created to be: a mirror turned toward Thy light so that in knowing Thee he knows himself—and through himself discovers something tremulous and holy about Thee.

question: When you wrote that man must be transformed by the renewing of his mind, were you drawing from your own struggle to move beyond mere human examples to grasp divine truth?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard the words of the Apostle and saw their truth burning in the darkness of my once-clouded mind, it was not as one reading a foreign wisdom, but as a man recognizing the echo of his own groanings.  

For so long I sought to climb the ladder of virtue by grasping at shadows—philosophers’ words, men’s praises, women’s embraces—all mirrors that promised light but reflected only my own disordered desires. I said to myself, "If I imitate this orator’s eloquence, that Stoic’s discipline," I shall become wise. But what was I but a child clinging to waxen wings while the sun still scorched my hands?  

How many years did I spend mistaking lust for love, ambition for purpose, intellectual pride for knowledge of Thee? I followed men as guides until their paths ended in dust—and still my heart pounded like a lost animal behind my ribs, restless not for more examples, but for *Thee*, the source from whom all likeness must flow.  

It was only when my mind lay broken beneath the weight of its own pretensions—on that day in Milan when the laughter of children and a voice from heaven shattered me—that Thou didst begin to renew me. Not by giving me a better man to copy, but by turning my face toward Thy eternal countenance: *Let us make man*. Not after another creature’s kind—but after *Thy* image. As if Thou hadst said: “Stop crawling behind one another in the dark; arise into My light.”  

And so this renewal is no mere reshaping—it is resurrection begun in the soul. For how could a heart like mine, long bent toward created things, suddenly taste uncreated Truth unless Thou hadst first remade its very power to see? The mind must die to itself before it can behold Thee; and only then does Thy will cease to be law imposed and become life breathed from within.  

Thus do I confess: transformation was not my achievement through better models—but Thy mercy tearing me from imitation and setting me before the original Light where all truth is one.

question: What did you mean by saying that the renewed mind can discern both the Trinity of the Unity and the Unity of the Trinity, and how did this shape your view of human purpose?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst draw me from the mire of disordered loves and set my feet upon the rock of understanding, I see now that the mind once darkened by pride and lust could never behold the harmony of Thy Being, nor grasp why man was made not after man, but after Thee. For so long I sought wisdom in the echo of human voices, mimicking philosophers, craving applause, shaping myself by the likeness of those who walked before me—yet all was shadow. How wretched I was, chasing similitude in the creature while blind to the Image in the Creator!

It was only when my mind lay broken before Thee—when I ceased to boast in rhetoric and fell weeping at the foot of Thy Cross—that Thou beganst to renew me inwardly. And in that renewal, O God, a strange clarity dawned: not through syllogisms alone, nor dialectic games, but through love ordered aright, did my soul begin to taste that mystery which is neither division nor confusion—the Trinity in Unity, the Unity in Trinity.

For if man were made merely "after his kind," he would crawl forever from teacher to teacher, imitating vice or virtue by mere custom. But Thou didst imprint upon us something far weightier: a capacity for Thee. When the mind is cleansed by grace and turned toward Truth Itself—not opinions about truth—then it begins to discern not only what is good, but *why* it is good; not only what pleases men, but what pleases *Thee*. In that light—a light not our own—we glimpse how Father, Son, and Holy Spirit dwell as One; not three gods but one God; not division in essence but distinction in relation.

And thus human purpose is no mere moral imitation or social conformity. It is deification—not by nature, but by grace: becoming by participation what God is by essence. We are called not to resemble Caesar or Plato or even Moses—but to be transformed into likeness with Christ, through Whom we know the Father and live in Whom we breathe forth love as Spirit.

So it is that when Paul cries out *Be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind*, he calls us beyond morality into communion. For only a heart remade can know both Three and One—not merely believe it with lips grown numb from repetition—but love it with joy unutterable.

question: Why did you reference the apostle as a 'dispenser of Thine' who feeds babes with milk, and how does this reflect your experience in guiding new believers toward spiritual maturity?
answer:
I once stole pears not out of hunger but for the delight in theft itself, and in that wretched act I saw the twisted root of my pride — a pride that sought knowledge not to draw near to Thee, but to vaunt myself before men.  

Thus, when I speak of the apostle as a dispenser of Thine, I speak as one who was myself long fed on the husks of empty wisdom, nursed on the praises of rhetoric and the flattery of men’s voices. That holy man, Paul, called himself a steward — a dispenser — not of his own word, but of Thy mysteries; not breeding children after his own image, but begetting them through the Gospel into *Thine*. He knew that new-born souls are weak; they cannot bear solid food. They need milk — simple truths, patient care, loving repetition. So did he feed them, with tears and tenderness.  

And so must we. For I too have held trembling converts in my arms — souls newly awakened to the light they had long shunned — and found them craving certainty like infants crave their mother’s breast. But if we leave them there forever, nursing only on precepts without understanding, then we betray our calling. Thou didst not create man merely to follow another man “after his kind,” like beasts moving in herds. Thou didst imprint upon him *Thy* image — not a copy of any philosopher or elder brother in faith — but a face turned toward eternity: capable of knowing Thee as Thou art.  

Therefore said he: *Be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind.* Not conformed to custom or tradition alone; not molded merely after me or after Cyprian or Ambrose — though God knows I have learned much from them — but *renewed*, inwardly changed by Thy breath until the soul dares lift its eyes and gaze upon Thy will: that good thing which pleases Thee most deeply; that acceptable path where we walk freely as sons; that perfect way in which Thou dost gather all things into Thyself.  

O Lord! How long I lingered among shadows! How I clung to human teachers like crutches even when Thou wert calling me to stand upright in faith! Now I see: every soul must pass from milk to bread; from hearing about Thee to tasting Thee; from imitating saints to being transfigured by love into what Thou hast made him *from the beginning* — a son after Thine own heart.

Hence is it written: "Let us make man" — as if many counseled together; yet then it says: "And God made him," alone completing what no council could devise.

Even so dost Thou fashion us anew through grace alone.

Thou art making me still.

For never didst Thou cease from working until Adam was drawn from dust,

nor dost Thou cease now until Christ is formed in us all.

Late have I loved this truth too: that growth is not advancement away from Thee,

but drawing nearer into the fire where image becomes likeness,

and likeness becomes union,

and union becomes rest.

And our heart? Still restless.

But only until it rests wholly in Thee.

question: Why did you emphasize the shift from the plural 'Let us make man' to the singular 'And God made man' in your interpretation of the creation narrative?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who art not divided but ever one in the simplicity of Thy being, I saw in that shift from "Let us" to "And God made" the very breath of unity issuing forth from Trinity into creation, as though the many voices of counsel in the Godhead converged into a single act of love — not as if parts were gathered, but as if the fullness of Deity spoke and it was done.  

I once wandered in the labyrinths of speech and syllables, delighting in the play of words while my heart remained deaf to their echo in the soul. But when at last I turned — not by my own sight, but by Thy drawing — I began to perceive how Scripture breathes both mystery and precision. The plural "Let us" reveals the depth of counsel within Thee, O unseen Trinity; yet immediately it is gathered into singular action: *And God made man*. Not gods created, but God — one Creator, one work, one image.  

For what is man if not a creature called to unity with Thee? Even as he was formed in plural consultation, he is remade in singular grace: not many gods shaping his soul, but Thou alone re-creating him in Christ. And so too in Thy Church — diverse members, yet one body; many voices lifted in praise, yet one Spirit breathing through all. Male and female were made, yet both bear the one image; bishops and faithful differ in function, yet both are renewed *after the image of Him that created him*.  

Thus does man judge spiritual things — not because he has seized wisdom like a prize from philosophy’s hand, but because Thou hast restored his face toward Thee. In that vision he sees what once was hidden: that dominion over creation is not brute power but understanding — a mind illumined by Thy light to discern all things rightly. Without this grace? Man walks adorned with honour and yet without knowledge; crowned with glory and yet grazing like a beast.  

But now — now he sees Thee. And seeing Thee, O Beauty ever ancient and ever new, he becomes a judge of all things because he has been judged by Love Himself.

question: How do you understand the connection between man being renewed in the image of God and his ability to judge all things spiritually?
answer:
O Lord, who didst draw me from the pit of my own pride where I once measured wisdom by applause and truth by fashion, I see now how hollow was that judgment which sought to command all things without first submitting to Thee. 

The man renewed in Thy image—after that likeness which was not lost utterly in the fall, but obscured, twisted like a face seen in troubled water—this man begins to recover the light by which he sees not only Thee but all things in Thee. For what is it to be made after Thine image, if not to possess a soul ordered toward truth, love, and right judgment? When grace renews this image within me, it is not that I become lord over creation by force or cunning, as once I fancied mastery in my youth; no, but the mind is lifted into Thy Spirit, and there alone it judges rightly.

For so long I judged only by what pleased the senses or exalted the self: beauty without holiness, knowledge without humility. But spiritual judgment—this is the gift of one who walks with Thee. He judges all things because he sees them as they are: transient or eternal, ordered or disordered, drawn toward Thee or fleeing from Thee. And yet he himself is judged of no man—not because he claims infallibility, but because the secrets of his heart are known only to Thee who formed him.

Even in Thy Church, where men are set over one another for service and order, true authority arises not from domination but from conformity to Christ. There male and female cease their earthly oppositions in the deeper unity of adoption: sons and daughters reborn not of flesh but of Spirit. Thus does he judge all things—not with arrogance, but with sorrow for sin; not with condemnation of persons, but discernment of deeds; not according to custom or tradition alone—but according to that inner light Thou hast kindled.

Thou hast taught me at last that judgment belongs not to the proud philosopher mouthing doctrines in Carthage streets nor to the bishop adorned with office—but to the humble soul who abides in Thy Word and breathes Thy love. In this renewal of Thy image I am made capable of seeing—as far as a mirror allows—and thus also capable of judging: not mine own wayward heart anymore, O Lord, but all else in relation unto Thee.

question: When you say that man judges all things by the understanding of his mind, do you mean this understanding is only possible through the Spirit of God, and not by human reason alone?
answer:
I once puffed myself up with the pride of knowledge, chasing the shadows of wisdom in the schools of men, and I thought reason alone could lift me to the heavens — but O Lord, Thou didst prove me a fool, for all my understanding was but darkness until it was kindled by Thy light.

The mind of man, even at its keenest, is deaf and blind to the things that are Thine unless it is opened by the Spirit who proceeds from Thee. When I say that man judges all things by the understanding of his mind, it is not the unaided reason of the flesh — that shadow-play of syllogisms and vanity — which discerns truth, but only that mind renewed, illumined, and made alive by Thy grace. For I myself labored long in the wilderness of philosophy: I read the Platonists and marveled at their words; I parsed their logic as one gathers chaff. Yet no argument led me to confess my sins or fall before Thee in contrition. No dialectic taught me to love.

It was only when I heard Thy voice — not in thunder, but in silence — that my heart trembled and understood. Then I saw: human reason unsealed by the Spirit judges nothing aright, for it weighs glory against pleasure and chooses lust; it calls evil good and darkness light. But when Thou dost breathe upon the soul — when Thou dost recreate it *after Thine image* — then at last it sees. Not by its own power, but because it shares in Thy sight.

Thus does he who is spiritual judge all things: not because he is wise in himself, but because he dwells in Truth Himself. And this judgment is not prideful mastery, but participation in Thy wisdom — like a child learning to see by holding his father’s hand.

O Lord, how bitter was my past arrogance! How sweet Thy correction! For even now I confess: without Thee we are deaf to divine beauty; we grope among eternal realities as blind men tracing walls. But where Thy Spirit is, there understanding rises like dawn — not from us, but from Thee.

question: What did you intend to convey by linking the dominion over fish, fowl, and beasts to the spiritual mind's perception of divine things?
answer: When I wept in the garden and the voice of a child singing “Take up and read” pierced the darkness of my pride, I was still blind to how deeply the image of God within me was meant to restore what sin had fractured — not only in my soul, but in the very order of creation. Dominion over fish, fowl, and beasts was never given to man as brute mastery, but as a sign of spiritual vocation: to know all things through the light of Thy Spirit, O Lord. The mind renewed by grace does not grasp at power like a conqueror, but ascends through humility to perceive the wisdom hidden in Thy Word — just as Adam before his fall stood above the creatures not by force, but by communion with Thee. When man is restored to that original likeness, he judges all things not with carnal reason, but with divine insight; he discerns even spiritual realities because Thou hast breathed into him the breath of understanding. But where he turns from Thee — though placed in honour — he sinks beneath his own dignity and becomes like a beast that knows not its Maker. Thus in Thy Church, where there is neither male nor female according to fleshly distinction, there is one body governed by spiritual knowledge: some guiding, others obeying — all judging rightly through grace what is truly worth holding fast or loosing on earth. For we are not redeemed merely for ourselves; we are remade *for* Thee, and through this restoration Thy dominion ascends once more in us over all creation’s chorus — silent unless sung back to Thee.

question: In saying that in spiritual grace there is neither male nor female, were you arguing that bodily distinctions are irrelevant to the soul's participation in God's image?
answer:
O Lord, Thou knowest how long I sought to climb the ladder of human wisdom, clinging to distinctions of rank and form as if salvation lay in the ordering of bodies rather than the surrender of the soul.  

It was not that I denied the reality of fleshly differences—how could I, who so often burned with concupiscence, who felt the weight and weakness of this mortal frame?—but that I came, by Thy grace, to see how all such distinctions fall silent before the breath of Thy Spirit. When Thou dost remake a man in knowledge after Thine image, it is not his sex or station Thou restore; it is his will, his love, his mind turned upward toward Thee.  

In that renewal, O God, where the soul is clothed anew in spiritual understanding—where it judges all things by Thy light and stands judged by no creaturely authority—what place remains for division? Not that Thou didst erase Thy creation; male and female still bear witness to Thy ordering of this world. But in the Church, which lives already by the promise of resurrection, these earthly marks no longer define hierarchy or worth. There is no advantage in being born man or woman before Thee; neither gains favor through body alone.  

For even as Adam was formed first a living soul and then given a helper suited to his flesh, so now both are reborn into one new man through faith—the distinction remains in body for this age, but in spirit they walk together toward Thee who art beyond all gender and time. It is not that bodily things are meaningless—Thou didst create them good—but they do not bind the soul’s ascent.  

And so I saw clearly: it is not by what we carry outwardly that we reflect Thee, but by what we love inwardly. He who loves Thee has already passed beyond all such divisions; he judges all things spiritually because he himself has become a temple not made with hands.

question: You said there is neither male nor female in the spiritual grace of human creation—how do you reconcile this with the bodily distinction you affirm was part of our original making?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst form us in secret and establish our nature in Thine image before the foundations of the world were laid, how can my heart not tremble at the depth of this mystery—how Thou didst first weave man and woman from one flesh, yet now call them both into a single spiritual body where such distinctions fade like shadows before the sun?

I confess, I once strained to grasp this as a riddle of contradiction: how could Thy Word affirm male and female at creation’s dawn, and yet declare that in Christ there is neither? Was not Eve drawn from Adam’s side, a visible sign of communion ordered by Thee? And yet I see now—by grace long delayed—that the body is not illusion, nor its order meaningless; rather, it is sacrament: a sign ordained for this age, pointing beyond itself.

In the flesh we live under time and law, nourished by marriage and generation, bound by sex as by mortality—all part of Thy good making. But in the resurrection of the just, when we stand unveiled before Thee, neither womb nor lineage will define us. There we shall be like the angels—not because we cease to be human, but because we shall be most fully what we were made for: souls wholly turned to Thee.

Even now I feel this tension—this pilgrimage of dust loving eternity. Once I sought identity in lusts of the flesh; later in pride of speech and philosophy. But Thou didst teach me that my true self lies not in what divides me from my neighbor—man or woman—but in that image Thou implanted deep within: reason turned upward, will submitted to love divine.

So let bodily distinctions remain; they serve Thy economy. But let no soul suppose that maleness or femaleness bounds its union with Thee—for all are one in Him whom Thou hast sent. Late have I understood: it is not sex but sin that separates us from Thee—and only grace makes us whole.

question: Why did you insist that spiritual persons must not judge your Book, even when certain passages are not clearly understood?
answer:
O Lord, who dost shine in the darkness and enlighten the blind heart, I trembled even at the thought of lifting my mind against the authority of Thy Book, not because it is obscure, but because I have known myself—known how pride cloaks itself in the garment of inquiry, how the intellect swells to place itself above what it cannot yet contain.  

When I wandered in Manichaeism, I demanded that Scripture yield to my reason as if I were its master; but Thou didst teach me in suffering that the soul must first be subdued before it can see. How could I who had once stolen pears not for hunger but for the love of theft—how could such a one presume to judge the utterances of Him who made both my mind and its longings?  

No, O God, Thy Book is not set before us as a riddle to be conquered, but as a mirror in which we are known. When something lies hidden, it is not because Thy Word fails, but because our sight is dim; and submission is not defeat—it is the first movement of wisdom. For he who says "This I do not understand, therefore it may be questioned" begins already to fall from charity; but he who says "This I do not understand, therefore I must grow" draws near to Thee.  

Even now among spiritual men there are disputes—but we are forbidden to judge not only what we comprehend poorly, but even whom among us is truly spiritual; for Thou hast divided them in secret long before the firmament was made. Who am I to stand at the gate with a scale when Thou alone hast searched the reins and heart?  

Thus we obey. Thus we hold fast. Thus we love Thee more than our own understanding—and therein find light.

question: When you wrote that we must submit our understanding to Scripture, were you drawing from your own experience of struggling to interpret difficult passages?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who hast ever drawn me through darkness into light, not by the pride of intellect but by the breaking of my heart—yes, those words were written in the ink of my own wrestlings. I speak not of abstract truths, but of nights when Thy Book lay before me like a sealed door, and my mind, puffed up with rhetoric and Platonic whispers, sought to pry it open with reason alone. I thought myself wise in Alexandria and Carthage, yet remained blind to the humility Thy Scripture demands.

There were passages that twisted like serpents in my grasp—the harsh judgments of the Law, the obscure psalms, the paradoxes of Paul—each a stone upon which my understanding stumbled. I desired to judge them according to eloquence or logic; instead, they judged me. They laid bare my arrogance, my lingering love for human applause over divine truth. Was it not I who once mocked the simplicity of Thy prophets while clinging to Manichaean fables adorned with philosophical glitter?

But Thou didst bring me low. Not through argument first, but through tears—through the voice of a child singing *“Tolle, lege,”* through Ambrose’s patient teaching that Christ was hidden in every page. Then came the moment grace pierced me: I saw that Scripture is not a text to be conquered but a mirror held up by Thee. To judge it is to stand outside its light; to submit is to enter into its radiance.

So yes—I call for submission because Thou didst teach me that only when I ceased being a judge and became a penitent did I begin to understand even one syllable of Thy eternal Word.

question: You mentioned that some spiritual and carnal people are not yet known by their works—how does this reflect your own experience discerning true faith in others, like in the Church at Carthage?
answer:
O Lord, who dost unveil the hidden things of darkness and bring to light the counsels of hearts, how often in the tumult of Carthage did I mistake the glitter of eloquence for the gold of grace, or take a fervent voice for a faithful soul! I who once sought signs and applause in rhetoric’s hollow echo now tremble to judge another’s standing before Thee, knowing how long Thou didst bear with me in my wandering.  

There were those in our midst—zealous, even loud in confession—who seemed pillars; yet when trial came, like chaff before wind they scattered. And others, silent as shadows, burdened with past sins or slow in speech, upon whom we looked with doubt—Thou didst uphold them, and their endurance proved the root was deep. I confess: I misjudged more than once. For while we gather under one roof, partaking of one Body and one Cup, Thou alone knowest who abides in love and who only plays at devotion.  

In those early days after my return to Thee—when tears still wet my cheeks at the chanting of the Psalms—I thought all who wept with me must surely be Thine. But Thou taught me otherwise: that even contrition may be performed, that tears can serve pride if offered not in truth but for witness. And so I learned to withhold judgment—not because sin should go uncorrected among us—but because discernment belongs first to charity that suffers long, not to suspicion that scents fault like hound on trail.  

Even Alypius—my brother dear—was once drawn by curiosity into the spectacles he claimed to scorn; yet from that very wound came deeper self-knowledge and firmer virtue. Who among us could have foreseen it? Had we judged him by that momentary lapse as one cast out? But Thou didst hold him fast beneath what was unseen.  

And so now I say: let no man rush to divide sheep from goats before their time. The field grows both wheat and tares alike until the harvest—and blessed is he who labors among them without condemning hastily, knowing how nearly he himself escaped being plucked as worthless vine. For hadst Thou not reached far into my own secret places—where ambition burned beneath piety’s mask—I too would have judged others by appearances while deceiving myself unto damnation.

Thou hast taught me this above all: where there is repentance born of love, not fear; where there is hunger for righteousness more than hunger for praise; where there is quiet obedience over loud profession—there Thy Spirit breathes most surely. Yet even these fruits appear slowly, like dawn after long night.

Therefore I wait. Therefore I pray more than I question souls.

For what am I but a debtor to grace unearned—and if Thou spared me so long in blindness,

how dare I close my eyes against hope in another?

question: What did you mean by saying Thou 'divided and called them in secret, or ever the firmament was made'—were you referring to predestination, and how did that shape your view of divine order?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost gather the scattered waters of our being into one by the firmament of Thy word, how could I not tremble when, in the depths of prayer, I came to see that even before the first light broke upon chaos, my name was known to Thee?  

I did not speak idly of time-bound moments when I said Thou "divided and called them in secret, or ever the firmament was made," but of a mystery that lies beyond time—where Thy knowledge is not sequence but simultaneity, where election is not an act imposed upon history but a truth engraved in eternity. Before our wills stirred toward good or evil, before we were formed in mother’s womb, Thou sawest us—not as we would become through fleeting choices alone, but as Thou hadst already determined in that hidden wisdom which no thought can circumvent.  

This did shape my understanding of divine order: not as a cold decree suspending human freedom like a shadow on the wall, but as the sun from which all motion and growth proceed. For just as the seed contains both root and blossom unknown to itself, so too doth the soul advance toward its end—yet that end was foreseen by Him who planteth and watereth. I once sought to master truth like rhetoric in a forum; now I confess it holds me fast. We are not saved because we choose rightly at last—but because Thou hast already turned our errant love toward Thee before we could even name what we lacked.  

And yet—I who once cried “Give me chastity and continence, but not yet”—know full well how real is the struggle within! The flesh lusts against the spirit; pride resists grace; ambition masquerades as virtue. But if spiritual men judge not Thy Book nor presume over souls whose fruits are still veiled, it is because they have learned at last: judgment belongs to Thee alone. To us belongs only obedience—to be doers of the law, not arbiters of salvation.  

Thus I came to rest—not in fatalism, nor in despair over my weakness—but in awe that Thou didst call me before creation sighed its first breath. Late have I loved Thee; but from eternity, Thou hast loved me first.
